prompt: A photorealistic oil painting of a master tailor's workshop, late afternoon light filtering through tall dusty windows. Along the back wall stand five or six standard dressmaker's mannequins, all roughly the same upright posture, draped in clean well-fitted suits — ordinary, transferable, made for anyone. In the center of the room, lit by a single focused lamp, stands one extraordinary mannequin. Its posture is wildly unusual — one shoulder dramatically higher, torso twisted slightly left, a subtle forward hunch, hips at an odd angle. And the suit on this mannequin is a masterpiece. Every seam follows the exact curve of this particular form. Every dart, every pleat, every lapel is sculpted to this one body with obsessive precision. It fits perfectly — and would be grotesque on anyone else. Scraps of fabric litter the floor around it. Dozens of discarded attempts hang on the wall nearby — each one slightly less extreme than the last. The tailor's tools are scattered across the workbench, suggesting this took everything. The standard mannequins in the background wear suits that fit well enough. The center mannequin wears something that fits only itself. No people, no text, no labels, no diagrams, no measuring tape with numbers. Dutch Golden Age interior style, oil on canvas texture, cinematic lighting, photorealistic.
metaphor: A suit tailored obsessively to one contorted mannequin fits it perfectly but would fail any other body — background mannequins in standard suits make the failure to generalize visible.
